# The year that changed the shape of Albanese's agenda

By chief digital political correspondent [Clare Armstrong](https://www.abc.net.au/news/clare-armstrong/105969816)

- [Topic:Government and Politics](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/government-and-politics)

5h ago5 hours agoSat 2 May 2026 at 3:00pm

![AUSTRALIAN FEDERAL ELECTIONS 2025](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/22254f9fbf804025fe9aded2790e4909?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=4644&cropW=8256&xPos=0&yPos=430&width=862&height=485)

Labor had secured a historic 94-seat majority in the lower house, simultaneously vanquishing the leaders of not one, but two, opposing political parties at the 2025 election.  (ABC News: Brendan Esposito)

One year ago, Anthony Albanese was a man on top of the world.

Before long, that political high would be cut down by a domestic terror attack, a new conflict in the Middle East and an oil-driven economic shock now reshaping Labor's second term.

Rapturous applause greeted the prime minister as he walked out onto the stage at the Canterbury-Hurlstone Park RSL Club in Sydney to victory in the 2025 federal election.

Labor had secured a historic 94-seat majority in the lower house, simultaneously vanquishing the leaders of not one, but two, opposing political parties.

In his speech that night, Albanese acknowledged the world had "thrown a lot at our country over the past three years".

There had been wars abroad, a global energy crisis and soaring inflation that had only just begun to show signs of moderating.

As he vowed to continue helping Australians under pressure, the Labor loyalists in the crowd could be forgiven for thinking Albanese's second term might prove more fertile ground for ambitious domestic reform.

![Anthony Albanese raises hands with son, partner and Penny Wong in front of a crowd.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e8eee32a7a43451c6d8dde0caeafce91?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2902&cropW=4353&xPos=116&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Rapturous applause greeted the prime minister as he walked out onto the stage at the Canterbury-Hurlstone Park RSL Club in Sydney to victory in the 2025 federal election.  (ABC News: Brendan Esposito)

## A tragically prescient observation

Even though there was still much uncertainty, Donald Trump had only just been re-elected as US president and the global conflicts were far from resolved, there was a distinct air of hope in the room.

No doubt that feeling only grew in the following months as the Coalition descended into chaos, effectively eschewing their opposition duties in favour of internal warring, and a tentative ceasefire began in Gaza.

It was Albanese, though, who in early December made what would prove to be a prescient observation.

"Inevitably … there will be issues arise that we can't foresee now," he told Insiders host David Speers.

Exactly a week later, Australia endured its deadliest terror attack on domestic soil.

The traumatic events at Bondi shocked the nation, with Albanese quickly becoming the lightning rod for much of the hurt and frustration ripping through the devastated Jewish Australian community.

Backflipping on his initial resistance to hold a royal commission and recalling parliament early to pass new gun control and hate laws went some way to steadying Labor's precarious political position.

The necessarily vague early findings of the royal commission interim report handed down this week were a reminder of how little time has really passed since Bondi, even if the attack seems a lifetime ago.

The US-Israeli strikes on Iran in late February and the war, global oil crisis and economic upheaval that have followed have forced Albanese into a constant state of reactive governing.

As the conflict drags on, anticipation about promises from Treasurer Jim Chalmers and Albanese that the federal budget will feature serious reforms to address intergenerational inequality has been replaced with scepticism.

Albanese's confirmation this week that Labor will not be making changes to export taxes on existing gas contracts surprised absolutely no-one.

Gas tax advocates are clinging onto the technicality that there is still potential for future reform or changes to future and uncontracted gas supply.

But most have accepted the energy crisis sparked by the Iran war and subsequent closure of the Strait of Hormuz has clearly killed off any chance of more substantial tax changes at this time.

Any lingering wishful thinking will surely be dashed by whatever further public assurances Albanese offers as he welcomes his Japanese counterpart, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, to Australia next week.

## Budget less than a fortnight away

While changes to the capital gains tax discount paired with some amendment to negative gearing arrangements remain in play, there is already a long line of economists mounting the argument that these two alone hardly constitute serious reform.

The inflation figures released this week showing prices rose 4.6 per cent in the 12 months to March, up from 3.7 per cent in the year to February, are only the beginning.

Chalmers said as much when he warned the Iran war "could drive inflation up even higher before it comes back down again".

A year ago, Labor won an election with a handy indirect assist from the Reserve Bank of Australia, which had just cut interest rates for the first time after 13 hikes.

Now, markets are pricing in a 75 per cent chance of an RBA rate rise this Tuesday, which would be the third increase this year.

Just as it was in the early budgets of its first term, Labor is again caught between pressure to deliver cost-of-living relief and also rein in spending to help tame inflation.

Even if the government cuts deep into public spending, something it is endeavouring to do with the NDIS changes announced last month, there is little that can be done to offset the widespread impact of high global oil prices.

A temporary fuel excise cut helps motorists at the bowser, but oil is a major input cost for plastics, groceries, construction, freight and many other sectors.

Successive economic shocks are also contributing to a now well-established trend of growing disaffection with Australia's major political parties.

## Labor not immune to One Nation

The Coalition has borne the brunt of this shift in the electorate in the past few federal cycles and will certainly be turfed out of the regional NSW seat of Farrer at Saturday's by-election.

But Labor is not immune to the threats posed by the advent of popular community independents and the rise of a resurgent One Nation.

The minor party snatched a chunk of Labor's primary vote in some key state seats at the South Australian election.

Federally, several Labor MPs ended up in two-party preferred counts against an independent challenger.

These factors are known to Albanese, who has taken time this weekend to reflect on his past year in a series of media interviews.

He told ABC Radio National's Saturday Extra program Australia was currently seeing a "breakdown in the system of norms that we could take for granted".

This, he said, includes falling support for free trade, a rise in protectionism and nationalism, of far-right populism and some corners of the far left.

"Some of the certainties that were there have been taken away from us," he said.

"And I think that understandably creates some anxiety in people."

Add to all that the "different role" now being played by the US, which Albanese said, "changes the dynamic for the world in western democracies".

In a short period of time, the optimism of the 2025 election night has collided with a far less forgiving reality.

For Albanese, his second term is now defined less by what he wants to do than by what unforeseen events will allow.

# Australian taxpayers also take on risk for major oil and gas projects. Why is that ignored?

By business reporter [Gareth Hutchens](https://www.abc.net.au/news/gareth-hutchens/12002796)

- [Topic:Oil and Gas](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/oil-and-gas)

5h ago5 hours agoSat 2 May 2026 at 2:58pm

![Close up of a collection of jagged red rocks. In the background, two towers spitting flames.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/cb42a8378f21f5b092b9185f51ff69f2?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2214&cropW=3936&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Australian governments, and taxpayers, often take on a lot of risk to get major resource projects up and running. (ABC News: Charlie Mclean)

Major energy companies are telling Australians that this country's oil and gas industry relies "entirely" on private sector investment.

In a recent social media post, the Australian Energy Producers made this claim:

> "You might have heard the claim that Australia should tax its oil and gas industry the same way Norway does. But does that comparison really stack up?
>
> "For starters, the Norwegian government is a major investor and co-owner of its oil and gas industry, whereas Australia’s oil and gas industry relies entirely on private sector investment.
>
> "That means, in Australia, the private sector — not government — wears all of the risk and up-front costs, which can be in the tens of billions for just one project."

In other words, the oil and gas lobby argues that Australian governments don't wear any risk in the development of our oil and gas industry and don't assume any up-front costs for major energy projects.

But do these claims stack up to scrutiny?

## An industry built on help from governments

[Australian Energy Producers](https://energyproducers.au/) (AEP) is the "peak" industry body representing Australia's oil and gas exploration and production sector.

Its [members](https://energyproducers.au/about/members) include Woodside Energy, Santos, Chevron, Shell, Origin, Inpex, BP, Hancock Prospecting, Exxon Mobil, and Halliburton, among others.

Many of those companies have well-oiled lobbying and PR operations that have been honed over decades because they know that "help from the government" is exactly what they need.

Take the North West Shelf project, which is Australia's largest oil and gas project.

In a submission to a [federal inquiry](https://treasury.gov.au/consultation/review-of-the-petroleum-resource-rent-tax) into the Petroleum Resource Rent Tax (PRRT) in 2016, Western Australia's government [had this to say](https://treasury.gov.au/sites/default/files/2019-03/R2016-001_Western-Australian-Government.pdf) about the North West Shelf:

> "In the 1970s and 1980s the state played a pivotal role in securing the development of the North West Shelf project through agreements, financial assistance and infrastructure provision.
>
> "If the state had not made these investments, the project would likely not have succeeded."

It said the North West Shelf project proceeded in two major stages.

The first stage, "the domestic stage," which got the project up and running, involved the production of natural gas, which the (then) State Electricity Commission of Western Australia (SECWA) promised to purchase.

SECWA was a WA government-owned entity. And it didn't promise to purchase some of the project's early gas output. It agreed to purchase _all_ of it.

Why would it do that? So that the North West Shelf project would have a guaranteed customer in its early years to ensure its economic viability.

As the WA government explained in its submission:

> "The domestic phase was underpinned by 20-year 'take or pay' contracts, signed in September 1980, between the Project Joint Venturers and SECWA for the supply of 414 tetrajoules per day of natural gas (commencing in 1985) — the entire gas output from the domestic phase.
>
> "These state agreements were essential in ensuring the financial robustness for the North West Shelf project and were key to securing contracts with Japanese utilities for the export phase."

So, state government support was essential, in the beginning, to get the project into a position where it was capable of exporting Australian gas to Japan.

The WA government also explained, in its submission, that the first phase of the project ran into major problems.

It said, in the early 1980s, that key players involved in the venture realised that the early contractual agreements would have to be modified because they were threatening to cause SECWA's financial collapse, which could stall (and potentially collapse) the North West Shelf project.

So in March 1985, the Commonwealth and WA governments and the joint venturers agreed to "share the pain" that was forecast to be borne by SECWA, to keep the whole project alive.

Why would the federal government agree to do that?

In his book [Inside the Hawke-Keating Government: A Cabinet Diary](https://www.mup.com.au/books/inside-the-hawke-keating-government/9780522866438) (2014), Gareth Evans has a story to tell about that.

## A North West Shelf 'bombshell'

Mr Evans was one of the major figures in the Hawke and Keating governments of the 1980s and 1990s. He was the minister for Resources and Energy from 1984 to 1987.

His diary entry on January 24, 1985 provides an insight into the politicking that surrounded the gas project at the time.

"A North West Shelf bombshell," his entry began.

"At a hastily arranged late-afternoon meeting in my Canberra office with the Woodside management, led by its managing director, Charles Allen, and chairman, together with Kevan Gosper as the Shell shareholder and a BHP executive as another major shareholder, they tell me a very unhappy story."

![Gareth Evans cabinet diary](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4769ee06c1c2959959ab079dcfd1435f?src)

Gareth Evans, "Inside the Hawke-Keating Government: A Cabinet Diary", 2014

According to Evans, the executives said they were struggling to secure financing for the project, and they needed to be able to tell a consortium of banks that the SECWA "take or pay" contract for domestic gas purchases would definitely be honoured.

But the WA government, led by premier Brian Burke, was playing a risky political game of brinkmanship, in a bid to get the federal government to tip in financially.

"The immediate Woodside objective is to get us to put pressure on the Burke government to give the necessary assurance, but they acknowledge that some greater role for the Commonwealth — including perhaps picking up the $500 million tab for the West Australian Gas Pipeline — may be required if the whole house of cards is not to collapse," Evans records.

"I indicate that I will raise it with the PM at the first opportunity."

A week later, Evans records that he gets word that the North West Shelf project was in danger of "imminent collapse" and Woodside directors (including Brian Loton from BHP and Kevan Gosper from Shell) were flying to Canberra again to see prime minister Bob Hawke and treasurer Paul Keating.

In another diary entry, he records an episode in which Paul Keating gave Woodside's executives "a fairly withering blast."

> "The general theme, which seems to me quite justified, is that they have known about the problem for years, done nothing to resolve it, have been too financially weak to deserve a place in this league, and now — despite all their private enterprise protestations — want the federal government to bail them out so that they can lick bowls of cream into the hereafter," Evans writes of Keating's reprimand.

He records that multiple long days of negotiations were held in February and March between the federal government, WA government, and North West Shelf venture partners, which eventually led to a compromise deal being struck to keep the project above water.

As part of the deal, the federal government agreed to forgo millions of dollars of royalties for a number of years.

## Who takes on the risk? Who pays the cost?

Let's return quickly to the WA government's submission to the PRRT inquiry in 2016.

As the submission detailed, the WA government supported the North West Shelf project in many other ways, besides the "take-or-pay" contracts.

It said state support included the construction, by SECWA, of the Dampier to Bunbury gas pipeline, at the cost of roughly $1.1 billion, to enable SECWA to deliver the gas it had promised to purchase to major markets in the south-west of WA.

Altogether, it said, WA's expenditure in support of the North West Shelf project was calculated in 2010 by the Western Australian Treasury to exceed $8 billion (in 2010 net present value terms).

> "In addition, the Western Australian government incurred costs relating to town site development, schools, hospitals, community facilities and roads that were either directly associated with the project or arising from the economic and population growth accompanying the project," it said.
>
> "These costs have never been officially aggregated."

What did Australian Energy Producers claim again?

It claimed (on [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/australian-energy-producers_you-might-have-heard-the-claim-that-australia-activity-7450430751067762688-sc8s/) and [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/share/v/1Rfo6U5CJE/)) that "Australia's oil and gas industry relies entirely on private sector investment" and that "in Australia, the private sector — not government — wears all of the risk and up-front costs."

I asked the AEP about those statements on Friday.

In a response, the AEP's chief executive Samantha McCulloch provided this clarified statement:

> "Unlike Norway and Qatar, Australian governments do not take an equity stake or invest directly in our oil and gas industry. Instead, we rely on the private sector to invest billions of dollars and carry the full commercial risk of projects.
>
> "More than $400 billion has been invested in Australia’s gas industry since 2010 and the sector is the second-biggest corporate taxpayer, paying $21.9 billion in taxes and royalties in 2024–25."

That's a noticeable amendment to the original public claim.

But consider that new line in that new argument that the private sector takes on "the full commercial risk" of projects.

Australian governments help to reduce the commercial risk of major projects by building pipelines and other infrastructure, by signing "take-or-pay" contracts that guarantee long-term demand at certain prices, and so on.

By placing that risk on taxpayers' shoulders, governments take a large amount of risk _off_ the gas industry.

It's why more voters seem to be wondering why Australian governments don't take an ownership stake in these projects.

## There's no such thing as a self-made billionaire

At any rate, the North West Shelf project is just one project in Australia. There are many others. When you look into the history of each one, you'll find similar stories.

Which raises a final point.

The AEP originally argued that the private sector in Australia, not governments, wears "all of the risk and up-front costs" of major projects. But that argument relies on a very narrow definition of "risk" and "costs."

What about the cultural costs?

When the North West Shelf site was being developed, between [4,000 to 5,000 ancient rock carvings](https://www.aph.gov.au/Parliamentary_Business/Committees/Senate/Environment_and_Communications/BurrupPeninusla/Report/c06) on the Burrup Peninsula were destroyed, and many others were relocated.

In 2020, Rio Tinto infamously [destroyed the 46,000-year-old](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2021-05-18/one-year-on-from-rio-tinos-juukan-gorge-blast/100145712), culturally significant rock shelters in WA's Pilbara so that it could expand its iron ore mine.

What about diplomatic risks and costs?

Do we remember that Australian intelligence officials bugged an East Timorese cabinet office in the early 2000s during negotiations over shared access to the Greater Sunrise oil and gas field, which sits between Australia and East Timor, and an oil and gas treaty worth tens of billions of dollars?

Which industries in Australia were going to benefit from that diplomatic behaviour?

What about the climate risk and the environmental costs?

As former Treasury secretary Ken Henry [said recently](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-26/ken-henry-tells-inquiry-to-tax-australia-gas-industry-more/106591686), Australia is among the top three fossil fuel exporters in the world, and the costs of burning those fossil fuels are being worn by everyone through climate change.

There's no such thing as a self-made billionaire. Private fortunes depend on the social structures and conditions that allow such fortunes to be accumulated.

And the financial success of Australia's export industries depends on the same principle.

# Commentary on Nepean By-election 2026

https://www.abc.net.au/news/elections/nepean-by-election-2026/commentary

Link copied

ShareShareresults

**By Chief Elections and Data Analyst [Casey Briggs](https://www.abc.net.au/news/casey-briggs/7300030)**

10:50pm

Counting is over for the night in Nepean, and with all the early votes now in, the Liberal primary is 38.5% (-9.6), and after prefs Anthony Marsh is on 63.5% (vs Tracee Hutchison). Marsh still wins easily even if One Nation stays in second place after Green votes are distributed.

8:40pm

The Victorian Liberal Party have retained the state seat of Nepean, on the Mornington Peninsula.

Anthony Marsh, the mayor of Mornington Peninsula Shire, will be the next member for Nepean.

He succeeds former deputy Liberal leader and tennis player Sam Groth who resigned from parliament in February.

8:07pm

Early votes are starting to come through (about 2,600 of the 20,000 votes so far), and they seem to be helping the Liberals and hurting One Nation.

Anthony Marsh has strengthened to around 38 per cent of the primary vote, and it has pushed One Nation down to just below 20 per cent.

Presuming Tracee Hutchison stays ahead of One Nation, the preference count between her and Anthony Marsh suggests the Liberals would then go on to win.

But this result has been very patchy. We’re yet to get any votes out of the Rosebud area, and we don’t know where the early votes reported so far have come from.

7:20pm

The first two polling places have just dropped, with 2,000-odd votes from the western side of Nepean.

The Liberals lead with 33.5 per cent of the primary vote in Blairgowrie and Rye, a swing against the party of nearly 12 percentage points.

Tracee Hutchison has 24 per cent, and One Nation has 20.6 per cent of the vote in those two booths. You’d have to guess that these will not be One Nation’s strongest booths.

6:10pm

Without Labor in the picture tonight, the big unknown is how the Liberals, One Nation, and independent Tracee Hutchison perform relative to each other.

**The electoral commission has chosen to count preferences between Hutchison and Liberal Anthony Marsh**. That’s just an indicative count for tonight, and they may or may not turn out to be the final two candidates in this race.

It also may complicate our ability to know who has won this election, if it turns out that One Nation will make it into the final two.

The commission will proceed with its Lib vs Hutchison count even if it’s clear Hutchison (or the Liberal, for that matter) will come third. In that scenario it would then be up to the commission to decide if it chooses to rethrow those preferences early next week, or if it will wait to conduct a full distribution of preferences.

2 May 2026, 5:20pm

Voting ends in just under an hour, and counting will begin soon after. We're expecting the VEC to count everything it has to hand tonight, including in-person early votes and all postal votes already received and processed.

In total, 23,605 people voted before today at early voting centres. That's about 2,000 short of 2022 total in Nepean, But the number of postal vote applications was up by several thousand to over 10,000.

About 60% of those postal votes have been returned and should be counted tonight, but if this race is close, the outcome could hinge on the remainder of those postal votes. Postal votes will be included as long as they're returned before Friday 8 May.

16 April 2026

One Nation's Darren Hercus drew the top position on the ballot in last week's ballot draw, with Liberal Anthony Marsh in fourth position, and independent Tracee Hutchison drawing seventh. Early voting kicks off next Wednesday, and most candidates have now lodged [how to vote cards with the electoral commission](https://www.vec.vic.gov.au/voting/current-elections/nepean-by-election/how-to-vote-cards).

The Liberals and One Nation are suggesting voters preference each other over Tracee Hutchison, while Hutchison has lodged an open ticket with no preference recommendations.

The Greens are suggesting voters give their second preference to Hutchison, while Legalise Cannabis also has an open ticket.

5 March 2026

Mornington Peninsula Shire Mayor [Anthony Marsh](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-02-28/vic-liberal-candidate-nepean-by-election/106400212) has emerged as the Liberal candidate for Nepean, beating three other preselection candidates inlcuding Nathan Conroy, the Liberal candidate for the federal seat of Dunkley in last year's by-election.

With Labor now announcing it will not contest this seat, attention turns to other potential challengers, including One Nation's declared candidate Darren Hercus.

The “Independents for Mornington Peninsula” group, which backed Ben Smith in the overlapping federal seat of Flinders at last year’s election, has been searching for a candidate and [has told local media](https://www.mpnews.com.au/2026/03/03/nepean-by-election-marsh-hits-the-campaign-trail-while-labor-sits-it-out/) that it will be running a candidate.

Nominations for this by-election will officially open on 16 March, and close on **10 April**.

18 February 2026

The by-election is now set down for **Saturday May 2**, which is only about a week earlier than the absolute last possible date permitted under state law. It pushes the election well clear of the South Australian state election, and gives independent candidates more time to organise themselves and mount a serious campaign against the Liberal Party. It also means that the new member for Nepean will sit in parliament for less than six months before the House of Assembly is dissolved for the state election.

16 February 2026

Sam Groth resigned from parliament on Friday, and the Speaker says a date for the by-election will be announced this week.

11 February 2026

Sam Groth has said he will resign from parliament this week, and then it will be up to the Speaker to determine a date for the by-election. Legislation requires a by-election to be held, even though the general election is only nine months away.

The Speaker has to issue the writ for the by-election within a month of Groth's resignation, with election day a minimum of 25 days later, meaning the earliest the by-election could be held is **14 March**. If the Speaker waits until mid-next week to call the election or opts for a longer campaign period, it will be delayed until at least **21 March**, the day of the South Australian election. For the government, there are political advantages to having this by-election clash with the SA election and tying up Liberal resources in Victoria.

For Labor, the first big question is whether to even nominate a candidate. Labor won this seat in Daniel Andrews' 2018 landslide win, but the party's statewide polling is nowhere near that level in 2026. The government may, as it did in the Prahran by-election last year, opt to sit this one out.

Independents didn't perform particularly well in Nepean in the 2022 state election, but Nepean sits inside the federal electorate of Flinders, in which independent Ben Smith won 21.2 per cent of the vote and narrowed the Liberal margin to 2.3 per cent.

The map below shows you the polling place results from the 2025 federal election, and you can toggle on the boundaries of the state seat of Nepean. Just on the election day vote, Ben Smith performed slightly better in the booths inside Nepean, than the booth outside.

interactive-electorateboundaries-2

We'll have more clarity on the prospects for this election once we know who is actually running.

# Anthony Marsh wins Nepean by-election, retaining seat for Liberal Party

- [By Melissa Brown](https://www.abc.net.au/news/melissa-brown/167028)
- [Topic:By-Elections](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/by-elections)

1h ago1 hours agoSat 2 May 2026 at 6:45am

![A man with short red hair in a blue jacket and white collared shirt smiles in a room with blue and white balloons and people.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/afecc219da15266276f3e3e4eda09e5b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Anthony Marsh has won the Nepean by-election, retaining the Victorian state seat for the Liberal Party. (ABC News: Patrick Rocca)

The Liberal Party has retained the Victorian state seat of Nepean,on the Mornington Peninsula.

The ABC's elections analyst Casey Briggs is projectingformer Mornington Shire Council mayor Anthony Marsh will be the next member for Nepean.

ABC News - Trusted Source of Latest News & Headlines

## Nepean VIC

Liberal retainLIB retain

**Anthony Marsh leads by 4,500 votes.**

10.2% swing to LIB

### Preference count

79.8% counted, updated 19m ago19 minutes agoSat 2 May 2026 at 7:42am

### Liberal Party

#### Anthony Marsh

Vote: 63.4%

10,638

### Independent

#### Tracee Hutchison

Vote: 36.6%

6,138

He succeeds former deputy Liberal leader and tennis player Sam Groth, who resigned from parliament in February.

"Mr Marsh's two main challengers, One Nation's Darren Hercus and independent Tracee Hutchison, both currently have just over 20 per cent of the primary vote," Briggs [wrote in his Nepean by-election commentary](https://www.abc.net.au/news/elections/nepean-by-election-2026/commentary) **.**

> "Marsh has around 40 per cent, and a preference count between Marsh and Hutchison shows the Liberals easily winning."

The Liberal Party has held the seat on the Mornington Peninsula, south of Melbourne, for all but one term over the past 40 years.

Mr Marsh was elected to local government as an independent in 2020, after spending time in the air force.

He also owns a small business.

Mr Marsh only joined the Liberal Party weeks before the by-election.

He had previously said he would not run for parliament but now says he is doing so due to the performance of the state Labor government.

![A person with yellow fingernail polish holds a leaflet that has a picture of a man and a woman and says "Vote Anthony Marsh".](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/cd2ae5ec09f83f13128d3939c6979ccb?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Anthony Marsh only joined the Liberal Party in the weeks before the election. (ABC News)

He was tipped to win, but analysts were watching for any challenge from One Nation.

The by-election comes just six months before the 2026 Victorian general election.

In his victory speech, Mr Marsh described becoming the Member for Nepean as "the honour of my life".

"I'll be on the ground each and every single day fighting for the people of Nepean as we lead into one of the most important state elections that we've seen."

![A man with short red hair, dark rimmed glasses in a blue blazer and white collared shirt holds a microphone, smiling.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/0ebe4dec21c0cfe6c70b7834a5a034fe?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Anthony Marsh claims victory speech in the Nepean by-election. (ABC News: Patrick Rocca)

Mr Marsh entered the Liberal gathering behind Opposition Leader Jess Wilson.

In introducing Mr Marsh, Opposition frontbencher David Southwick said it had been a tough campaign but that the result was good news ahead of the November state election.

"The people of Nepean have said yes for Jess," he said.

![A woman with chin length blonde hair in a dark jacket and white shirt holds a microphone in front of a Liberal Victoria sign.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/33b12da1e9eaa227f243df61b405a336?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=135&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Opposition leader Jess Wilson described Anthony Marsh as a passionate local representative. (ABC News: Patrick Rocca)

Ms Wilson accused Labor of neglecting the electorate.

"The government did not even run a candidate here," she said.

"That demonstrates how little they care about people here on the peninsula."

# King Charles won over Donald Trump with his US visit but not progressive mayor Zohran Mamdani

- [![A close up photo of a brunette woman wearing glasses.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c175b85d1638aaf6c37a1c463fdb8fc0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=3333&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
L\\
\\
By Lucia Stein](https://www.abc.net.au/news/lucia-stein/7093484)
- [Topic:Royalty](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/royalty)

5h ago5 hours agoSat 2 May 2026 at 2:56pm

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 55 seconds.

Watch 55s

![New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani speaks to press from podium at New York City Hall with supporters behind him..](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6fc206250ba3b567350db9ea32126f68?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Zohran Mamdani reveals what he would say to King Charles if they spoke.

The United States pulled out all the stops for King Charles's state visit with President Donald Trump.

Dinner was served on plates encrusted with pure gold. The royal family members were heaped with praise. And the King became only the second British monarch to deliver an address to Congress, after his mother, Queen Elizabeth II, in 1991.

But not everyone was gripped by royal fever.

The newly installed Democratic socialist mayor of New York City, Zohran Mamdani, was elected on a pledge to help the working class.

And on Wednesday (local time) in the city that never sleeps, Mamdani showed exactly what that would look like when it came time to meet the King.

![Zohran Mamdani and New York Governor Kathy Hochul speak at a podium.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/b519bbeb3de465d60bf90a14d92efdc0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Zohran Mamdani said if he were to speak to the king, he would "probably encourage him to return the Koh-i-Noor diamond". (Reuters: Shannon Stapleton)

The pair both attended a wreath-laying ceremony in the city to honour victims of the 9/11 terrorist attack, but instead of rolling out the red carpet for the monarch, Mamdani's team said the two men would not be meeting privately afterwards.

His spokesman, Joe Calvello, [told the New York Times](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/04/29/us/mamdani-diamond-king-charles.html) the mayor was "generally opposed to the idea of a king" and when Mamdani was later asked what he would say to Charles if they were to spend time together outside of the ceremony, he didn't mince his words.

"I would probably encourage him to return the Koh-i-Noor diamond," he told reporters.

Mamdani was referencing the most expensive diamond in the world, a precious stone surrendered by — or stolen from, depending on which side is telling the story — a young Indian prince and presented to Queen Victoria in 1850.

Born in Uganda to Indian parents who dedicated their lives to examining colonialism, Mamdani has been more than willing to speak on the issue in the past, most notably when discussing the resistance of Ireland to Britain's "colonial project" in [a St Patrick's day speech in March](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/17/nyregion/mamdani-st-patricks-day-palestinians.html).

With his latest comments he has again shifted attention from the pomp and circumstance of a calculated royal visit to the unsettled question of the Koh-i-Noor's ownership and Britain's colonial legacy.

## The debate over a centuries-old diamond

British royal legend says the diamond will bring bad luck to any man who wears it.

Nowadays it mostly sits on display in the Tower of London, set in the front cross of the Queen Mother's Crown.

But many Indians say it belongs to them and have been calling for its return for decades.

In 2023, after the death of Queen Elizabeth II, those appeals surfaced on social media and reached a fever pitch in the lead up to Charles's coronation.

Queen Camilla chose not to wear the crown with the Koh-i-Noor at the event, opting to reuse another instead, apparently in an effort to avoid scrutiny.

But Mamdani's comments indicate the subject has not been forgotten.

Believed to have originated in the Golconda mines in central southern India, the "Mountain of Light" has a long and complex history.

After it was discovered it was claimed by Mughal Islamic emperors, Sikh maharajahs and emirs of Afghanistan before it ended up in the hands of an 11-year-old Sikh maharajah in the 19th century.

Duleep Singh was leading modern-day Punjab, which was colonised by Britain after two bloody wars that killed thousands, when he signed a peace deal that led to the gem being handed to Queen Victoria.

It has been the subject of myth and intrigue ever since.

"Agatha Christie wrote a book about it and everybody believed it \[was stolen\]. It wasn't," Melbourne-based jeweller and author Gary Holloway said.

"It survived World War II, \[then\] it was moved from the Tower of London to Windsor Castle … in a hat box along with a lot of other very famous diamonds, part of the Crown Jewels.

"It was also hidden in a hole dug under the castle walls. Eventually it became part of the Crown Jewels."

![A crown encrusted with diamonds, ermine fur and purple velvet, with a massive square diamond on the front](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d864bd16fced398fa55fd43a345574d2?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=654&cropW=491&xPos=461&yPos=133&width=862&height=1149)

Once adorning Queen Mary's crown, the Kohinoor was set in the front middle cross pattée.  (Wikimedia Commons: Cyril Davenport )

Mr Holloway said multiple countries with connections to the eighth largest diamond in the world appealed to have the British surrender it.

"It's been kidnapped all through its history," he said.

"Iran wants it, Pakistan wants it, Afghanistan wants it.

> "The fact that multiple invaders owned it means India is just one in the chain of people who claim it."

Since 2000, some Indian politicians have been calling on Britain to return the Koh-i-Noor, seeing its loss as symbolic of the country's subjugation under colonial rule.

"Britain owes us. But instead of returning the evidence of their rapacity to their rightful owners, the British are flaunting the Koh-i-Noor on the Queen Mother's crown in the Tower of London," prominent Indian MP Shashi Tharoor has said.

But just like the debate over countless other treasures taken from other countries during colonial wars that were subsequently put on display in the British Museum, the diamond controversy has not been easily solved.

Royal watchers have pointed out that [King Charles doesn't technically own the Koh-i-Noor](https://www.cbsnews.com/newyork/news/zohran-mamdani-king-charles-koh-i-noor-diamond/), and therefore has no more power to return the diamond than he has to hand Buckingham Palace over to the people.

## Mamdani snub not the only headline-making moment

King Charles's visit to the United States marked the 250th anniversary of its independence and was designed to help repair the country's "special relationship" with the UK.

Ties between Mr Trump and UK Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer rapidly deteriorated this year because of the latter's reluctance to join the war in Iran.

It was thought King Charles could help smooth things over while in the US, given Mr Trump's interest in the royals.

The president's excitement in the lead up to the visit indicated there was merit in the idea. But the trip was not without hiccups.

![Queen Camilla, King Charles, Donald Trump and Melania Trump wave from the White House portico.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/0c2f27ae49cdeb01ebefd87b293ef750?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=1&width=862&height=575)

Donald and Melania Trump (right) welcomed King Charles and Queen Camilla to the White House last week. (Reuters: Kevin Lamarque)

Before King Charles touched down in America, maintenance crews plastered lampposts near the White House [with the Australian flag](https://www.newyorker.com/news/the-lede/dc-gets-a-king-it-actually-wants) instead of the Union Jack.

More consequential were calls from [some in parliament](https://x.com/EdwardJDavey/status/2047586374123966745) for King Charles to cancel the trip after Mr Trump threatened to hit Britain with more tariffs.

Then there was a report in the Financial Times, which obtained audio of remarks made by the new British ambassador to the US, Christian Turner.

Mr Turner said it was "extraordinary" to him that the scandal over Jeffrey Epstein's powerful associates had "brought down a senior member of the royal family, a British ambassador to Washington, potentially the prime minister, and yet here in the US it really hasn't touched anybody".

The comments followed calls for the King to meet with Epstein survivors on this trip, and for him to reference them in his speech to Congress. Charles opted to do neither.

What the King did do was mention three of [Mr Trump's most hot-button political issues](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-29/king-charles-speech-to-congress-key-takeaways/106618704) in the most closely watched event of the visit: his historic address on Capitol Hill to a joint meeting of the Senate and House of Representatives.

NATO, Ukraine and climate change were touched on in quick succession, but Mr Trump didn't seem to mind, describing Charles as an "elegant man" once admired by his late mother.

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 1 minute 42 seconds.

Watch 1m 42s

![King Charles, wearing a gray pin-striped suit and a teal tie, looks at a pieces of paper, while speaking on a podium](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/18aa71071bc47b0f97db9f19c97a8ceb?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

King Charles said America's words "carry weight and meaning" and its actions "matter even more".

"My mother had a crush on Charles. Can you believe it? Amazing how … I wonder what she's thinking right now," the president said earlier in the week.

Charles, for his part, allowed the president to flatter him. He joked with him at a state dinner on Tuesday about the 1814 British firebombing of the White House and a battle between Britain and France to control North America in the 18th century.

"If it wasn't for us, you'd be speaking French," he quipped.

In his speech to Congress, the King alluded to recent problems in UK-US relations, telling the audience that "we can perhaps agree that we do not always agree".

He also pointed out the two countries had an enduring partnership "born out of dispute".

Meanwhile, Mr Trump avoided mention of any policy disagreements with the Starmer government and largely stuck to script, except when he suggested, without evidence, the King "agrees" with him [that Iran should not have nuclear weapons](https://time.com/article/2026/04/29/trump-king-charles-iran-war-claim-buckingham-palace-response/).

But does this mean the special relationship between the US and the UK is repaired?

## Trump says 'we'll find out' about mending ties

If history is anything to go by, the special relationship between the UK and the US will not be so easily fixed.

![Donald Trump and Keir Starmer, both in dark suits, stand side by side in front of the US and UK flags.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/dd4bd1292cc252c5157f09e35f4b4407?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Donald Trump (left) and Sir Keir Starmer at the White House in February 2025. (Reuters: Kevin Lamarque)

Last year Mr Trump stood next to Sir Keir at a White House press conference and praised him for his "beautiful" accent.

He has since [mocked](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/apr/03/trump-claims-starmer-weak-mocks-pm-uk-aircraft-carriers-iran-war) his British counterpart, labelled him a "coward" who was "not Winston Churchill" and said he was "not happy with the UK" over its stance on the Iran war.

"It's very sad to see that the relationship is obviously not what it was," Mr Trump told The Sun newspaper last month.

When asked if Charles's visit could improve relations, the US president told the BBC "absolutely, the answer is yes \[it could help relations\]".

But he appeared to walk that back in comments he made to [Sky News](https://news.sky.com/story/us-state-visit-latest-donald-trump-hosts-king-charles-as-uk-pins-hope-to-repair-relationship-13531757) last week.

"I can't give you an answer to that," he said.

> "I can tell you one thing, our relationship is very good, but you know, that's another individual. We'll find out."

He added that Sir Keir "has to learn to deal the way \[King Charles\] deals, and he'll do a lot better".

Certainly both men have had to manage the fallout of similar controversies of late, but what draws Trump to the King — the status of being a royal, his family and their lifestyles — is not easily replicated.

In the meantime, many issues between the two countries remain, including the ongoing fallout of the Iran war and reports the US drafted a [proposal to withdraw support for British sovereignty in the Falklands](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/apr/24/could-donald-trump-withdraw-us-support-uk-sovereignty-falklands).

At the very least, Sir Keir might be hoping the monarch's visit and soft diplomacy efforts will ease Mr Trump's ire in the short-term.

# Trucking up the Hume as diesel prices boom

On the Hume Highway, it takes four shots of coffee, $2,553 of fuel and a little hair metal to keep the wheels of the Australian economy turning.

But fuel costs mean the truckies’ next delivery could be higher prices at the check-out.

By [Margaret Burin](https://www.abc.net.au/news/margaret-burin/4218078) and [Andie Noonan](https://www.abc.net.au/news/andie-noonan/6559670)

- [Story Lab](https://www.abc.net.au/news/interactives/)

5h ago5 hours agoSat 2 May 2026 at 2:46pm

Sean Mulvaney climbs into his royal blue sleeper cab, named Whiskers.

This is his home away from home.

As one of 11 long-haul drivers for a small Victorian logistics company, Sean makes the 880-kilometre trip from Melbourne to Sydney a couple of times a week, transporting everything from dog food and e-waste to mattresses and PVC pipes.

Today, it’s full of fibreglass sheets.

[Sean Mulvaney fills up ahead of his trip.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619524) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[The price of diesel has soared since the Iran war began.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619592) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[It costs $2,000 for Sean to get to Sydney and back.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/null)

Sean and Whiskers are on the frontline of the fuel crisis as the global transport industry grapples with soaring costs stemming from war in the Middle East.

And as the biggest consumer of diesel in the world per capita, Australia is feeling the strain.

Sean’s just said goodbye to his partner, Liz, who likes to drop him off at his truck before a long-haul trip.

“It gives me that grounded home feeling before I say ‘see ya’ for six days,” he says.

[Sean farewells his partner, Liz.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619394) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Sean has been driving for Woody's Transport for five years.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619414) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

First, he needs to fill up his 42-tonne semi. He used to do it the night before, but with [rising fuel theft](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-14/fuel-theft-is-rising-as-diesel-prices-surge/106564956), trucks can’t be left full overnight.

The bowser clicks.

$1,135.17.

That might sound like a lot, but that’s only two of the four tanks. For the trip to and from Sydney, Sean adds fuel from the other side of the truck too.

The total bill is $2,553.75 for 983 litres of diesel, almost double what it was just a couple of months ago.

To fill Whiskers from empty, it would cost nearly $4,000.

Sean works for Milton Wood, who prides himself on the bushranger and Australiana artworks that adorn his trucks and make them stand out on the road.

[Milton 'Woody' Wood is an owner and driver of Woody's Transport based in Campbellfield.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625512) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Milton Wood says trucking companies have been hit hard.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619964) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

Milton says his company, Woody’s Transport, paid $250,000 for fuel last month — up from $140,000 the month prior.

Part of the cost is passed on to customers through a levy, but Milton is covering some of the additional expense for fear of losing clients; some have already cancelled jobs.

“On a Melbourne to Sydney trip, we’re probably still out of pocket about $250 on the fuel. But I’m just wearing that to work in with my customers and hopefully we all get through it.”

Right now, diesel prices along the Hume vary from $2.50 up to about $2.70.

The Fair Work Commission has [ordered companies that use truck drivers regularly to hold twice-monthly meetings to review rates](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-20/fair-work-commission-order-on-subcontractors-deal-/106584968). But in the meantime, trucking companies have been hit hard.

[Milton 'Woody' Wood owns Woody's Transport, based in the Melbourne suburb of Campbellfield.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619730) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Milton's trucks have plenty of details.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619670) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Woody's Transport vehicles stand out on the road.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619660) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Milton prides himself on the artworks that adorn his trucks.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619692) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Last month Woody’s Transport paid $250,000 for fuel — up from $140,000 the previous month.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619710) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

“They were pretty slow to act on it,” Milton says, “and even what they’ve done has barely touched.

“We had to find $100,000 to cover fuel before the customer pays for the job that’s been done … It all happened very quickly, but we needed the government to move quickly too.”

* * *

It’ll take about nine hours for Sean to get to Sydney. He’s one of about 5,000 heavy vehicles making this journey each day.

It’s the little things that break up Sean’s long-haul trips, which truck stops have clean toilets and showers, where he can get a freshly cooked meal and of course, where the coffee is good.

His standard is a maxi flat white, a gargantuan cup filled with four shots. Sometimes he orders a second, extra hot, for the road.

[Sean Mulvaney gets a large coffee before hitting the road.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619916) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Sean takes pride in his truck.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619888) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Sean driving.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/null)

Since the 1800s, the Hume has been a vital corridor between the nation’s two biggest economies.

Sean’s been tackling this journey for the past five years after taking a break from trucking while he had children.

He’s content in his own company and the peace of his cab. Well, it’s peace for him.

Right now, Def Leppard is on repeat.

“I love it. I just have the music on and I just sing … really out of tune,” he says. “If the windscreen could tell you how bad a singer I was, it’d be right.”

[The Hume Highway is a vital corridor between the nation's two biggest economies.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625062) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Drivers work to strict schedules to meet tight delivery deadlines.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625398) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

On the way to Sydney, Sean’s required to have two 15-minute breaks as part of Australian safety regulations and he has to log every time he arrives and leaves.

Sometimes he meets old friends during breaks along the route.

“We’ve all got grey hair and are divorced. It is tough being on the road.

“When you catch up with them, you catch up years and years of stuff in 10 minutes.”

That’s partly because there’s not much time for a chat; drivers work to tight schedules.

About halfway along the journey at Tarcutta, there’s a memorial dedicated to the dozens of truck drivers who’ve lost their lives doing their job, a reminder of the high stakes.

Just last week, one of the other drivers in his company was involved in a major collision where both trucks burst into flames, closing southbound lanes of the highway for more than 12 hours.

The risks are always on Sean’s mind.

“There’s always that fear that someone’s going to come through the trees coming at you. It’s always the fear factor.”

[There is a memorial in Tarcutta for truck drivers who have lost their lives.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106620272) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Sean is required to take two short breaks during his trip.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106620294) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[During the week, this truck stop is one of the busiest along the Hume.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625124) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[The risks of being a truck driver is always on Sean Mulvaney's mind.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106624960) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

In the next stretch, he’ll pass Picton.

In the 1970s, about 200 drivers blockaded part of the highway here for nine days, calling for an end to road taxes and threatening Sydney with weeks of food shortages.

Sean says he doesn’t believe many everyday drivers really think about the bigger picture when they pass truckies on the road.

“I think it’s quite important being a truck driver, bringing everyone’s freight, because what everyone touches or have got in their possession has been on a truck at one stage or another.

“Everything has been on a truck at one point in time.”

Sean’s route is one very small part of a vast and interconnected network of freight routes that distribute goods to our huge country.

Everything from food to electrical goods to building materials relies on trucking.

There’s long been talk about the need for better rail transportation to reduce the reliance on the highway.

[It will take about nine hours for Sean to get to Sydney.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/null)

[Milton Wood says trucking businesses are facing tough times.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625410) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Sean makes the trip between Sydney and Melbourne a couple times a week.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625490) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Many of us don't think about the thousands of trucks that make this journey every day.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625502) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

Phil Bullock, a private consultant in transport and logistics at NineSquared, says current fuel prices show how important it is for governments to invest in all areas of the supply chain, including rail.

“Freight is invisible when it works, but it’s painfully obvious when it doesn’t,” he says.

“There’ll always be a core market for road because the rail network will never be able to offer the breadth of distribution that the road network can offer.

“It’s not about one or the other. It’s about how the different modes can work together effectively.”

One often-mooted alternative is electric-powered trucks; their sales hit a high in March, but still only account for 1 per cent of overall purchases.

In China, the uptake of electric and hybrid vehicles has been much faster and electric truck sales now account for more than one-quarter of the total.

“It may be that government needs to work closely with the vehicle manufacturers and the freight operators to look at what could really increase the rate of take up,” Bullock says.

[ODYs\_NorthSouth\_2904](https://www.abc.net.au/news/null)

[ODYs\_HumeHighway\_2904](https://www.abc.net.au/news/null)

The higher cost of fuel is starting to trickle through to the check-out. Since the start of the year, a 1-litre bottle of full cream milk in Coles and Woolies has jumped 12 per cent, from $1.65 to $1.85 in April.

“The cost of fuel is having a significant impact on the day-to-day life of ordinary Australians,” says Dr Kushneel Prakash from the Melbourne Institute of Applied Economics and Social Research.

“Fuel in this modern economy … is actually the lifeline. It’s like a blood flowing through a human system.”

[Sean Mulvaney says there is "always the fear factor" when it comes to driving trucks.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106619984) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

Milton Wood fears things are only going to get worse for trucking companies until the oil crisis fully stabilises. And he’s not alone.

“We’re saying roughly 70, 75 per cent of operators won’t survive the six months, and it’s as high as 80 per cent for the 12 months,” Warren Clark from the National Road Transport Association says.

He says the federal government’s initiatives to help truck drivers with interest-free loans, reducing the fuel excise and the heavy road road user charge are welcome, but he wants changes to the road user charge extended until diesel is under $2 a litre.

“At this stage, we’ve heard from a number of our members and general industry people that they have parked up their trucks, that they have actually gone and sought work elsewhere, just earning wages until fuel prices can settle down.

“Until we can get some certainty in the Strait \[of Hormuz\], it’s probably going to be a tough time for industry.”

Dr Prakash says fuel prices are unlikely to fall as quickly as they rose.

“In economics, we call this the ‘rocket and feathers’ phenomenon, meaning it goes up drastically but when it’s time for this particular price to come down, it drops down like a feather.”

Of course, the biggest challenges facing truckies right now were created by leaders and events a world away from the Hume Highway.

“It’s not any of our war or our concern apart from it serves the world … oil for our diesel — which is really, really hard,” Sean says.

[Milton Wood fears things are only going to get worse for trucking companies until the oil crisis fully stabilises.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625356) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[One of the biggest problems trucking companies face is having enough money coming in to cover the increased costs.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625292) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[Sean stops to log his required breaks.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625242) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

[More than 80 per cent of household goods are transported by road in Australia.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/106625572) _(ABC News: Margaret Burin)_

But for now, Sean is still happiest when he’s on the road.

“I love driving trucks.

“I got into driving trucks at 18, absolutely loved it. You felt like the king of the road being in such an important piece of equipment driving across Australia and I really hope the industry stays alive and kicking.”

And right now, he’s looking forward to returning home to see Liz and his kids.

“When you get back and get that first cuddle, it’s all worth it.”

# US to withdraw 5,000 troops from Germany amid Trump spat with Merz

- [Topic:World Politics](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/world-politics)

5h ago5 hours agoFri 1 May 2026 at 8:55pm

![A man with dark suit, white shirt and red tie, smiles. He's standing to the left of the picture against a dark black background.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/55d3e1accf7347ca7b4c5d82f5c98ee5?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=95&width=862&height=485)

A Pentagon official says Donald Trump is reacting to "counterproductive remarks" from Germany. (AP: Matt Rourke)

## In short:

The United States will withdraw about 5,000 troops from Germany over the coming months, the Pentagon has announced.

US President Donald Trump had threatened to withdraw some troops from the NATO ally earlier this week after German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said the Iranian leadership was humiliating the US.

The Pentagon said the withdrawal was expected to be completed over the next six to 12 months.

The United States is withdrawing 5,000 troops from NATO ally Germany as a rift over the Iran war widens between President Donald Trump and Europe.

Mr Trump had threatened a drawdown in forces earlier this week after sparring with German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, who said on Monday the Iranians were humiliating the US in talks to end the two-month-old war

Mr Merz also said he did not see what exit strategy Washington was pursuing.

A senior Pentagon official, speaking on condition of anonymity, said recent German rhetoric had been "inappropriate and unhelpful".

"The president is rightly reacting to these counterproductive remarks," the official said.

Announcing the withdrawal on Friday, local time, the Pentagon said it was expected to be completed over the next six to 12 months.

Germany is home to some 35,000 active-duty US military personnel, more than anywhere else in Europe.

The official said the drawdown would bring US troop levels in Europe back to roughly pre-2022 levels, before Russia's invasion of Ukraine triggered a build-up by then-president Joe Biden.

The official also cast the decision in terms of the Trump administration's push for Europe to become the main security provider on the continent.

## German ties fray

Mr Trump has singled out Germany even as he has chastised other NATO allies for not sending their navies to help open the Strait of Hormuz during the conflict.

The waterway, a choke point for global oil shipments, has remained virtually shut, causing market turmoil and unprecedented disruption in energy supplies.

Mr Merz has said Germans and Europeans were not consulted before the US and Israel started attacking Iran on February 28, and that he had conveyed his scepticism about the conflict directly to Mr Trump afterwards.

"The president has been very clear about his frustrations about our allies' rhetoric and failure to provide support for US operations that benefit them," the senior Pentagon official said.

Mr Trump has long wanted to reduce the US troop presence in Germany.

He pushed for a reduction at the end of his first term, but that cut was never enacted.

Mr Trump lost the election and Mr Biden reversed the plan.

Mr Trump's announcement on Wednesday that he was reviewing US troop levels in Germany surprised German military officials who spoke to Reuters, citing what they called constructive meetings at the Pentagon earlier in the day.

They argue that Germany has done more than other allies to support the US war in Iran, including by allowing the use of bases and giving permission for overflights.

Germany is also home to a huge military hospital in Landstuhl.

As part of Mr Trump's withdrawal decision, a brigade combat team now in Germany will be pulled out of the country and a long-range fires battalion that the Biden administration had planned to begin deploying to Germany later this year will no longer deploy, the official said.

**Reuters**

# Charges yet to be laid as Kumanjayi Little Baby's family thanks community for support

[Olivana Lathouris](https://www.abc.net.au/news/olivana-lathouris/103566246) and [Grace Atta](https://www.abc.net.au/news/grace-atta/103443744)

- [Topic:Indigenous Australians](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/indigenous-australians)

15m ago15 minutes agoSat 2 May 2026 at 3:47am

![A teddy bear among floral tributes](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/53bec9065cf6f233e485b08d5d1be95f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=320&width=862&height=485)

Tributes to Kumanjayi Little Baby left outside the Old Timers / Ilyperenye town camp in Alice Springs where the five-year-old was last seen. (ABC News: Will Green)

## In short:

Robin Granites, the grandfather of Kumanjayi Little Baby, says the family is grateful for community support after the five-year-old's body was found on Thursday.

Alice Springs residents have laid tributes outside Old Timers town camp and dropped donations of food and blankets at Hidden Valley, where the family is holding sorry business.

## What's next?

Charges are expected to be laid against suspect Jefferson Lewis in the coming days.

The family of five-year-old Kumanjayi Little Baby, whose body was found on the outskirts of Alice Springs on Thursday, has thanked the community for their support as tributes flow in from across the country.

**Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander readers are advised this article contains the image of an Indigenous person who has died, used with the permission of her family.**

Hundreds of volunteers and police spent five days combing dense scrubland for the child after she went missing from a home at Old Timers / Ilyperenye town camp on the outskirts of the town last weekend.

![Young girl in pink dress does peace sign with fingers ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/3b3b39d1affbb726978d0aec40cd4bdb?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=335&cropW=502&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Kumanjayi went missing from a town camp in Alice Springs last weekend. (Supplied: NT Police Force)

Senior Warlpiri Elder and kinship grandfather of Kumanjayi Little Baby, Robin Japanangka Granites, said the family was appreciative of the efforts from authorities and volunteers.

"We're really grateful … it made us think we have people who really supported us," he said.

Northern Territory police arrested 47-year-old Jefferson Lewis on Thursday night in relation to the child's death, however charges are yet to be laid.

Mr Granites reiterated calls for community members to remain calm following an outburst of violence in the aftermath of Mr Lewis's arrest.

![Robin sits outside the town camp](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4de0c74087f86d60feadb7c0568ab24d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Robin Granites said Kumanjayi's family was grateful for community support. (ABC News: Xavier Martin)

"It is now time for sorry business and show respect for our family and to have a space and time to have grieving and remembering of her," Mr Granites said.

"Everyone is feeling very upset and emotion is very high.

"We are feeling angry and hurt of what has happened.

"We must let justice take its course."

## Pink tributes flood community

Alice Springs residents have laid pink flowers, teddy bears and messages of condolence outside Old Timers town camp to show support for the grieving family.

![Flowers on a fence with a note reading fly high baby girl](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/7a6578f40fd77559be4f9d301cc10a93?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Many people have left tributes to Kumanjayi Little Baby outside Old Timers / Ilyperenye town camp. (ABC News: Will Green)

A sunset vigil is expected to be held next week, with attendees encouraged to wear pink in honour of Kumanjyai Little Baby's favourite colour.

Locals have also been dropping off donations out the front of Hidden Valley town camp where members of Kumanjayi Little Baby's family are carrying out sorry business.

Mr Granites said he was "very thankful for those people" who have been providing food, clothes and blankets.

> "It's good because they know that we are in a sorry \[business\], and they know that they are our friends, our family, and all together we are one,"

he said.

![A man in a grey t shirt](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4f8d96d0a70e23de6f7e2a498cb0e33c?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Joshua Keomuongchamh helped in the search for Kumanjayi Little Baby. (ABC News: Will Green)

Joshua Keomuongchamh was among hundreds of volunteers who helped in the search for the girl earlier this week.

"As the days went on, we had the feeling of going into recovery mode," Mr Keomuongchamh said.

"As a father of five daughters, having granddaughter, the thought of finding a little five-year-old body is absolutely heart-wrenching."

Mr Keomuongchamh said he hoped the community could remain united in the weeks ahead.

![Flowers line a chain link fence](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e6fe4b845e5b57c3bb7509be4890ddb4?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The community has been coming together to mourn the loss of the little girl. (ABC News: Will Green)

"Hopefully the community comes together in recovery in the same way we came together to find her," he said.

There have been reports of unverified Go Fund Me pages being set up on social media however Secretariat of National Aboriginal and Islander Child Care (SNAICC) has established an official donation portal to provide direct funds to the family.

The member for Gwoja Chansey Paech is also taking donations for the family at his office in the main mall of Alice Springs.

## Suspect yet to be charged

Mr Lewis was transported from Alice Springs to Darwin on Friday morning for safety reasons.

NT Police Commissioner Martin Dole said despite being subjected to a "sustained assault", Mr Lewis's injuries were not significant enough to warrant him staying in hospital.

The 47-year-old was taken into police custody to be interviewed, and charges are expected to be laid in coming days.
